summary: This notebook implements a fast, physics-driven pipeline for estimating exoplanet transit depths and uncertainties from Ariel mission detector data. It processes raw signals through sensor-specific calibration, applies temporal binning and inverse-variance weighting, and detects transit phases using gradient analysis. A custom polynomial fitting routine optimized via Nelder-Mead estimates the transit depth, while per-planet uncertainties are dynamically derived from out-of-transit and in-transit variance ratios. Readers should learn how to efficiently parallelize detector calibration, retain hot pixels to preserve signal integrity, and replace fixed error estimates with data-driven multipliers for more realistic uncertainty quantification.
competition_flow: Reads raw detector parquet files and calibration masks, applies sensor-specific calibration and binning, detects transit phases via gradient analysis, optimizes transit depth using Nelder-Mead with polynomial fitting, estimates per-planet uncertainties from variance ratios, and outputs a formatted CSV submission.
data_reading: Loads signal, dark, dead, flat, and linear_corr arrays from per-planet sensor parquet files using pd.read_parquet.
Reads adc_info, test_star_info, and sample_submission from CSV files using pd.read_csv.
data_processing: Applies ADC gain/offset correction, non-negative clamping, and polynomial linearity correction.
Performs dark subtraction with alternating integration time scaling and flat-field division (excluding dead pixels but retaining hot pixels).
Crops sensor-specific ROIs, applies temporal binning, and computes inverse-variance weights across wavelengths with percentile clipping.
Uses Savitzky-Golay filtering for phase detection and winsorization for AIRS channel stability.
insights: Hot pixels should be monitored but intentionally retained in the data rather than masked to avoid losing valid signal.
Dynamic uncertainty estimation using out-of-transit and in-transit variance ratios provides more realistic error bars than fixed sigma values.
Inverse-variance weighting with percentile clipping prevents dominant channels from skewing the binned signal.
Parallel processing with pqdm significantly accelerates per-planet sensor calibration.
critical_findings: The author explicitly notes that hot pixels are intentionally not masked, contrary to typical practice, to preserve signal integrity.
Fixed sigma values are replaced with data-driven multipliers clipped to ±20-25% of the baseline to prevent overconfident or overly conservative uncertainty estimates.
